description: Diagnosis and management of chronic hypertension, gestational hypertension, pre-eclampsia and eclampsia.

sba.stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 34 weeks' gestation attends the day assessment unit with a blood pressure of 152/98 mmHg on two occasions 4 hours apart. Urine protein:creatinine ratio is 45 mg/mmol. She is asymptomatic and fetal assessment is normal.
sba.lead: What is the most appropriate first-line antihypertensive agent?
sba.rationale: She has pre-eclampsia with sustained hypertension above 140/90 mmHg. Oral labetalol is first-line unless contraindicated (e.g. asthma). Nifedipine is the usual second-line agent and methyldopa third-line. Magnesium sulphate is for eclampsia prophylaxis, not blood pressure control.

sba.stem: A 36-year-old woman with chronic hypertension is planning pregnancy. She currently takes ramipril.
sba.lead: What is the most appropriate advice about her medication?
options: Continue ramipril until 12 weeks then switch
Continue ramipril throughout at a reduced dose
Stop ramipril only if proteinuria develops
Switch to labetalol before conception or as soon as pregnancy is confirmed
sba.rationale: ACE inhibitors are associated with congenital malformation and fetal renal impairment. Switch to a pregnancy-safe agent before conception, or within 2 working days of confirming pregnancy. Stopping treatment entirely leaves chronic hypertension uncontrolled.
sba.hook: ACE inhibitor + pregnancy plan -> switch pre-conception.

sba.stem: A 31-year-old woman at 29 weeks' gestation admitted with pre-eclampsia has a generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 90 seconds. Intravenous access is in place.
sba.lead: What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Intravenous magnesium sulphate 4 g over 5-15 minutes
sba.rationale: This is eclampsia. First-line treatment is magnesium sulphate: a 4 g IV loading dose over 5-15 minutes then 1 g/hour for 24 hours after the last seizure or delivery. Benzodiazepines and phenytoin are not first-line; delivery follows maternal stabilisation.
sba.hook: Eclamptic seizure -> magnesium sulphate 4 g load.

sba.stem: A woman receiving a magnesium sulphate infusion for severe pre-eclampsia becomes drowsy. Respiratory rate is 8/min and patellar reflexes are absent.
sba.lead: What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
options: Stop the infusion and give 10 ml of 10% calcium gluconate IV
Halve the infusion rate and recheck magnesium levels
Intubation and ventilation without other measures
sba.rationale: Loss of deep tendon reflexes then respiratory depression indicates magnesium toxicity. Stop the infusion and give 10 ml of 10% calcium gluconate by slow IV injection as the antidote, with airway support as needed.
sba.hook: Mg toxicity -> stop infusion + calcium gluconate.

sba.stem: A 26-year-old primigravida at 12 weeks' gestation is assessed as high risk of pre-eclampsia because of chronic kidney disease.
sba.lead: What is the most appropriate prophylactic intervention?
options: Aspirin 75-150 mg daily from 12 weeks until birth
Low molecular weight heparin from 12 weeks
sba.rationale: Women with one high-risk factor (CKD, chronic hypertension, diabetes, autoimmune disease, or previous hypertensive disease of pregnancy) should take aspirin 75-150 mg daily from 12 weeks until birth. Antioxidant vitamins do not prevent pre-eclampsia.
sba.hook: One high-risk factor -> aspirin from 12 weeks.

sba.stem: A 33-year-old woman with gestational hypertension delivered vaginally 5 days ago and is breastfeeding. Her blood pressure is 158/102 mmHg.
sba.lead: Which antihypertensive is most appropriate to commence?
options: Amlodipine is the only safe choice
sba.rationale: Enalapril is a first-line postnatal antihypertensive, including for breastfeeding women, with monitoring of renal function. ARBs such as losartan are avoided while breastfeeding, and methyldopa is avoided postnatally due to its association with depression.

emq.theme: Classification of hypertensive disorders in pregnancy
emq.instruction: For each woman, select the SINGLE most likely diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

stems.stem: A woman with known hypertension since before pregnancy develops new proteinuria and rising blood pressure at 30 weeks.
stems.rationale: Chronic hypertension plus new-onset proteinuria/end-organ features after 20 weeks defines superimposed pre-eclampsia.
stems.hook: Chronic HTN + new proteinuria -> superimposed pre-eclampsia.

stems.stem: New hypertension at 33 weeks with proteinuria, epigastric pain, a platelet count of 88 and ALT of 140.
stems.rationale: Haemolysis, elevated liver enzymes and low platelets on a background of pre-eclampsia defines HELLP syndrome.
stems.hook: Low platelets + high ALT + epigastric pain -> HELLP.

stems.stem: New hypertension of 146/94 mmHg at 32 weeks with no proteinuria and normal bloods.
stems.rationale: New hypertension after 20 weeks without proteinuria or other features is gestational hypertension.
stems.hook: New HTN after 20 wk, no proteinuria -> gestational.

emq.instruction: For each clinical situation, select the SINGLE most appropriate drug or action. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

stems.stem: A woman with severe asthma needs a first-line oral antihypertensive for newly diagnosed pre-eclampsia.
stems.rationale: Labetalol is relatively contraindicated in asthma, so nifedipine is the preferred first-line oral agent here.

stems.stem: A woman with BP 172/116 mmHg and visual disturbance needs urgent control on the labour ward.
stems.rationale: Severe hypertension needs rapid control; IV labetalol (or IV hydralazine, or oral nifedipine) is appropriate, with magnesium sulphate for seizure prophylaxis.
stems.hook: Severe HTN -> IV labetalol for rapid control.

stems.stem: Prophylaxis against seizures in a woman with severe pre-eclampsia.
stems.rationale: Magnesium sulphate is used for eclampsia prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia, not for blood pressure control.
